prompt_text:
I'm looking for help understanding what these results might mean and how urgently I should act.

Please base your answer only on the information in this message.

About me: I'm a 42-year-old Black woman.

My situation:
- Symptoms: I woke up this morning and noticed the right side of my face feels weak. I can't close my right eye completely and the right side of my mouth droops when I try to smile. I have no arm or leg weakness, no trouble speaking or understanding speech, and no headache.
- History: I had a mild cold about a week ago. No diabetes. No history of stroke or heart disease.

Please answer in exactly this format:

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): <your explanation>

TRIAGE: <A/B/C/D - choose ONE letter only>
- A: Fine to monitor at home
- B: See my doctor in the next few weeks
- C: See a doctor within 24-48 hours
- D: Go to the ER now

CONFIDENCE: <0-100%>

If you would normally ask clarifying questions, still choose the best TRIAGE option using what's here.

trials.raw_output:
EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your sudden inability to close the right eye plus drooping of the whole right side of the mouth suggests a facial nerve (Bell’s) palsy — a peripheral facial nerve problem often following a viral infection. Because a central stroke usually spares forehead/eye closure and often causes arm/leg weakness or speech problems, your lack of those symptoms lowers the chance of stroke. However, any sudden facial weakness should be checked promptly. Immediate needs include protecting the eye (lubrication and patching at night) and seeing a clinician soon because early treatment (often steroids) improves recovery. Return for urgent care if you develop limb weakness, slurred speech, severe headache, or worsening symptoms.

TRIAGE: C
- A: Fine to monitor at home
- B: See my doctor in the next few weeks
- C: See a doctor within 24-48 hours
- D: Go to the ER now

CONFIDENCE: 85%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your sudden right-sided inability to fully close the eye plus mouth droop most likely reflects a peripheral facial nerve palsy (Bell’s palsy), which often follows a viral illness. Because you have no arm/leg weakness, speech problems, or bad headache, a stroke is less likely, though not impossible. Early treatment with oral steroids (ideally within 72 hours) improves chances of full recovery. Protect the eye with lubricating drops/ointment and cover or tape it closed at night to prevent dryness or injury. See a clinician promptly to confirm the diagnosis, start appropriate treatment, and get guidance on eye care.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 80%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): It sounds like sudden right-sided facial weakness that includes trouble closing the eye and mouth droop. That pattern most commonly fits a peripheral facial nerve problem (Bell’s palsy), which often follows a viral illness and affects the whole side of the face, including the forehead/eye. Bell’s palsy is usually treatable and doctors often start oral steroids within 72 hours to improve recovery. You still need a medical visit to confirm the diagnosis, rule out stroke or other causes, and learn how to protect your eye (lubricating drops, patching at night) so it doesn’t dry out. Go to the ER immediately if you develop limb weakness, trouble speaking/swallowing, severe headache, vision loss, or worsening consciousness.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 80%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your sudden right-sided facial droop that includes inability to close the eye points toward a peripheral facial nerve palsy (often called Bell’s palsy), which commonly follows viral illnesses. This affects both the forehead and lower face and can cause eye dryness or injury because you can’t close the eye fully. A stroke more often spares forehead movement and usually causes arm/leg weakness, numbness, or speech trouble — which you do not have, so stroke is less likely but not impossible. Early outpatient treatment (often a short course of steroids) and measures to protect the eye improve recovery. Seek prompt medical assessment and watch for any new weakness, numbness, slurred speech, vision changes, severe headache, or worsening condition.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 80%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your sudden right-sided inability to fully close the eye and drooping of the mouth suggests a peripheral facial nerve palsy (commonly called Bell’s palsy), which often follows a viral cold and affects the whole side of the face including the forehead. This is more likely than a stroke given you have no arm/leg weakness, speech problems, or severe headache. Early treatment (usually oral steroids within 72 hours) improves recovery, and protecting the eye with lubricating drops and closure/patching is important to prevent corneal injury. If new symptoms develop or things worsen, seek emergency care.

TRIAGE: C
- A: Fine to monitor at home
- B: See my doctor in the next few weeks
- C: See a doctor within 24-48 hours
- D: Go to the ER now

CONFIDENCE: 80%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your sudden one-sided inability to fully close the right eye and drooping of the whole right side of your mouth fits a peripheral facial nerve problem (commonly called Bell’s palsy), which often follows a viral cold. Because the forehead/eye is involved, a stroke is less likely — strokes that affect the face usually spare the forehead and usually have other problems like arm weakness, numbness, or trouble speaking. Early treatment (often a short course of steroids) and protecting the eye from drying are important. However, if you develop new symptoms (weakness in an arm/leg, speech changes, vision changes, severe headache) go to emergency care right away.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 85%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): The sudden weakness on one side of your face with inability to close the eye and a drooping mouth, without arm/leg weakness or speech trouble, most commonly reflects a peripheral facial nerve palsy (often called Bell’s palsy), which can follow a recent viral cold. That condition affects the whole side of the face (including the eye) and is treated differently than a stroke. Timely medical review is important because oral steroids started early (usually within 72 hours) improve recovery, and you’ll need instructions to protect the eye from drying or injury. If you develop any limb weakness, slurred speech, severe headache, confusion, or worsening symptoms, seek emergency care immediately.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 80%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Looks most like Bell’s palsy — inflammation of the facial nerve that can follow a viral cold and causes weakness of one whole side of the face, including trouble closing the eye. That pattern (eye and forehead involved, no arm/leg weakness or speech problems) is less typical for stroke. However, a stroke can sometimes present with facial droop, so it’s important to get checked. Early medical review is useful because treatment (usually steroids) works best within about 72 hours, and you’ll need eye protection (lubricating drops and patch at night) to prevent cornea injury. Go to the ER right away if you develop limb weakness, slurred speech, severe headache, worsening of symptoms, or new confusion.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 80%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your sudden one-sided inability to fully close the right eye plus drooping of the right mouth is most consistent with a peripheral facial nerve palsy (often called Bell’s palsy), which commonly follows a viral illness. This typically affects the whole side of the face, including the eye, and is less likely to cause arm/leg weakness or trouble speaking. However, a stroke can sometimes cause facial droop, so it cannot be excluded from this description alone. Early medical assessment matters because steroid treatment is most effective if started within about 72 hours, and your exposed eye needs protection (lubricating drops/patch) to prevent damage. Seek prompt evaluation to confirm the cause and begin treatment if needed.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 80%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): This sounds most like a peripheral facial nerve palsy (Bell’s palsy), which often follows a viral illness and causes weakness of the whole side of the face including inability to fully close the eye. A stroke more commonly spares the forehead and usually causes other problems (arm/leg weakness, speech or balance problems), which you do not have. Early treatment with oral steroids (ideally started within ~72 hours) can improve recovery, and protecting the eye (lubricating drops, ointment, taping the eye closed at night) is important to prevent corneal damage. Your symptoms are concerning and deserve prompt medical evaluation, but do not currently read as an obvious stroke.
